2024] refers to the process of producing vivid and engag-

ing narrated audio works that align with multi-modal  in-
put instructions. This technology has numerous applications
in daily life, such as providing barrier-free reading experi-
ences [Nadeau and Craig, 2024], enabling information access
for visually impaired individuals [Rigaud et al., 2024], and
facilitating personalized expression [Rong and Liu, 2025].
Thus, developing human-like audiobooks that convey rich
emotions and paralinguistic information is crucial and nec-
essary.
In general,  text-to-speech (TTS) generation is a main
step in the audiobook generation.  In the literature, great
progress has been achieved by prior work [Du et al., 2024a;
Chen et al., 2024b; Du et al., 2024b; Xie et al., 2024]. How-
ever, directly applying these methods to audiobook genera-
tion suffers from three challenges: (1) Individual TTS model
lacks scene adaptability and often excels in handling only
a limited set of scenarios, failing to meet the diverse needs
of users.  In fact, current TTS models often possess varying
strengths and focuses due to their specialized training and ar-
chitectures. A single well-trained model cannot satisfy vari-
ous human requirements, and relying solely on one model is
typically not optimal.  (2) Current methods struggle to gen-
erate dialogue that incorporates multiple emotional states and
often lack a human-like expressive quality. Specifically, when
speech involves complex emotions, models tend to produce
an “averaged emotion” instead of accurately capturing the
difference. Additionally, the generated speech lacks paralin-
guistic features,  resulting in machine-like outputs that fail
to align with human preferences.  (3) There is still a lack
of an automated human-aligned evaluation framework. Cur-
rent methods are either automated but misaligned with human
preferences or rely on costly, time-consuming human evalua-
tions.
Therefore, in this work, the main question we aim to ad-
dress is: How to develop an emotional-rich and human-like
audiobook generation framework with a unified evaluation
framework that effectively satisfies various scenarios?
To address the above challenges, we propose a unified
training-free system, named Dopamine Audiobook, for both
emotional and human-like audiobook generation,  as well
as automatic human-aligned evaluation.  Our key idea is
to effectively prompt a multimodal  large language model
(MLLM)  into an AI agent  that acts as a “speech artist,”
capable of expressing and evaluating speech according to

2.1  Text-to-Speech Generation
Existing TTS methods  can  be  categorized  into  non-
autoregressive (NAR) models [Eskimez et al., 2024; Chen et
al., 2024b] and autoregressive (AR) language models [Du et

al., 2024a; Du et al., 2024b; Zhou et al., 2024]. E2-TTS [Es-
kimez et al., 2024] and F5-TTS [Chen et al., 2024b] con-
vert text inputs into character sequences with filler tokens,
achieving strong performance in zero-shot voice cloning.
CosyVoice [Du et al.,  2024a] adopts supervised seman-
tic tokens to improve alignment between semantic informa-
tion and text. VoxInstruct [Zhou et al., 2024] introduces a
new approach that combines content prompts and descrip-
tion prompts, extending traditional TTS tasks into a general
instruction-to-speech framework.  Each model exhibits dif-
ferent strengths and weaknesses in various TTS scenarios.
These specialized features underscore the need to explore
how to integrate their collective potential.
2.2  AI Agent
Some studies employ MLLMs as the core of AI agents to en-
able intelligent planning and decision-making, tackling more
complex tasks such as image generation and editing [Wang et
al., 2024b; Liu et al., 2024b], video generation [Tu et al.,
2024; Li et al., 2024],  tool usage [Gao et al., 2024], au-
tonomous driving [Fourati et al., 2024], and software devel-
opment [Qian et al., 2023].
Although MLLM agents have been applied  to many
tasks,  their use in speech generation is still  limited.  Au-
dioGPT [Huang et  al.,  2024a]  connects multiple audio
foundation models  to handle speech, music,  and sound
understanding and generation  in multi-round dialogues.
WavCraft [Liang et al., 2024] leverages LLMs to integrate
different specialized models for audio creation and editing.
These studies focus on using AI agents to enable systems to
perform diverse tasks. WavJourney [Liu et al., 2023] intro-
duces a compositional audio creation framework that gener-
ates speech, music, and sound effects in sequence, although
each component still needs further improvement. Recently,

cast audio and support interaction with the podcast, but it
mainly focuses on podcast content and lacks robust support
for multilingual, personalized, and emotionally expressive
speech generation.
In contrast, our approach targets emotional, human-like au-
diobook creation, supports multiple input modalities to en-
hance creativity, and enables automatic, human-aligned eval-
uation without manual intervention.

generates long speech with superior emotional expres-
sion, outperforming SOTA TTS models in quality, natu-
ralness, timbre matching, and emotionality. In the eval-
uation,  the Dopamine Audiobook demonstrates better
alignment with human preferences and can be trans-
ferred to various audio tasks.
2  Related Work
2.1  Text-to-Speech Generation
Existing TTS methods  can  be  categorized  into  non-
autoregressive (NAR) models [Eskimez et al., 2024; Chen et
al., 2024b] and autoregressive (AR) language models [Du et

al., 2024a; Du et al., 2024b; Zhou et al., 2024]. E2-TTS [Es-
kimez et al., 2024] and F5-TTS [Chen et al., 2024b] con-
vert text inputs into character sequences with filler tokens,
achieving strong performance in zero-shot voice cloning.
CosyVoice [Du et al.,  2024a] adopts supervised seman-
tic tokens to improve alignment between semantic informa-
tion and text. VoxInstruct [Zhou et al., 2024] introduces a
new approach that combines content prompts and descrip-
tion prompts, extending traditional TTS tasks into a general
instruction-to-speech framework.  Each model exhibits dif-
ferent strengths and weaknesses in various TTS scenarios.
These specialized features underscore the need to explore
how to integrate their collective potential.
2.2  AI Agent
Some studies employ MLLMs as the core of AI agents to en-
able intelligent planning and decision-making, tackling more
complex tasks such as image generation and editing [Wang et
al., 2024b; Liu et al., 2024b], video generation [Tu et al.,
2024; Li et al., 2024],  tool usage [Gao et al., 2024], au-
tonomous driving [Fourati et al., 2024], and software devel-
opment [Qian et al., 2023].
Although MLLM agents have been applied  to many
tasks,  their use in speech generation is still  limited.  Au-
dioGPT [Huang et  al.,  2024a]  connects multiple audio
foundation models  to handle speech, music,  and sound
understanding and generation  in multi-round dialogues.
WavCraft [Liang et al., 2024] leverages LLMs to integrate
different specialized models for audio creation and editing.
These studies focus on using AI agents to enable systems to
perform diverse tasks. WavJourney [Liu et al., 2023] intro-
duces a compositional audio creation framework that gener-
ates speech, music, and sound effects in sequence, although
each component still needs further improvement. Recently,
1
NotebookLM can convert user-uploaded content into pod-
cast audio and support interaction with the podcast, but it
mainly focuses on podcast content and lacks robust support
for multilingual, personalized, and emotionally expressive
speech generation.
In contrast, our approach targets emotional, human-like au-
diobook creation, supports multiple input modalities to en-
hance creativity, and enables automatic, human-aligned eval-
uation without manual intervention.
3  Dopamine Audiobook
Our proposed Dopamine Audiobook develops the MLLM
agent as an “intelligent brain” to coordinate and manage the
entire process, as shown in Figure 1. The generation process
includes two key novel components: Adaptive Capability-
based Model Selection and Emotional Enhancement Method.
3.1  Adaptive Capability-based Model Selection
Manually coordinating different models requires extensive
expertise, raising the barrier to entry and potentially hinder-
ing the timely discovery of satisfactory solutions. To address
this, we propose the Adaptive Capability-based Model Selec-
tion module, which adaptively selects the most suitable model

It consists of two steps: Prior Knowledge Injection and Model Selection. Prior Knowledge Injection We integrates the latest open-sourced models into our tool li- brary to support various TTS tasks across different scenarios, including F5-TTS [Chen et al., 2024b], CosyVoice [Du et al., 2024a], CosyVoice 2 [Du et al., 2024b], VoxInstruct [Zhou et 2 al., 2024], and MetaVoice . We evaluate and categorize the strengths of each model. Specifically, F5-TTS excels in zero- shot voice cloning, VoxInstruct allows control over speech style and emphasis through descriptions, CosyVoice shows robustness for multilingual speech generation, CosyVoice 2 is particularly effective for generating Chinese dialects, and MetaVoice accurately clones emotions from English speech prompts. These statistics provide detailed capabilities of the candidate models, enabling the agent to more precisely match the user’s current needs with the most suitable model. Model Selection Different models exhibit varying performance across TTS generation scenarios, with some having inherent limitations in controllability and language support. Therefore, we first enable the agent to filter models based on the target text’s lan- guage, excluding those that cannot handle it. Subsequently, the agent selects models based on the emotions in the target text and the retrieved reference speech prompts. Specifically, when the emotions in the target text align with those in the reference speech prompt and no emotional shift is present, 2 https://github.com/metavoiceio/metavoice-src eneration Stage 3：Speaker Planning & Retrieval Timbre Planning noon, little monkey • A Narrator: A man speaking at a steady pace with a relaxed ere playing outside. voice, conveying a sense of engaging storytelling. rs look extra fluffy • George: A lively young girl, with a slightly immature voice, full of curiosity and excitement. compliment, • Rex: A confident young man, with a tone that exudes ease as infectious as ever. and humor, and emotions tinged with a hint of pride. adventure today? Otherwise, the agent prioritizes the model’s controllability in speech generation. 3.2 Emotional Enhancement Method To further enhance the human-likeness and emotional expres- sion of the generated speech, we propose emotional enhance- ment methods that include two strategies: paralinguistic aug- mentation at the word level and prosody retrieval at the utter- ance level. Word-level Paralinguisitc Augmentation Paralinguistic features (e.g., breathing, emphasis, and laugh- ter) enable more fine-grained emotional expression. The ex- isting CosyVoice 2 [Du et al., 2024b] model can generate specified paralinguistic information. In this case, for each emotion-level sub-sentence, we allow the agent to plan ap- propriate paralinguistic features based on the paralinguistic library and incorporate them as text into the transcript. Ad- ditionally, the agent generates a text description of the emo- tion for each sub-sentence, which can be used as input for description-driven TTS or as retrieval targets for prosody re- trieval. Utterance-level Prosody Retrieval Compared to text descriptions, reference speech provides a more specific and direct prosodic template.

Inspired by
[Chen et al., 2024a], we propose a retrieval-driven prosody
enhancement strategy. Specifically, we construct an emotion-
rich text-speech pair dataset as our retrieval database, where
the speech clearly expresses the emotion corresponding to the.

Our proposed GPT-based evaluation framework, as shown
in Figure 2, consists of three key innovations: MagicEmo
prompt,  self-critique, and perspective-taking, enabling the
GPT to be human-aligned, self-aligned, and task-reinforced.
4.1 MagicEmo Prompt
For humans, dopamine levels increase when receiving re-
wards or engaging in positive social interactions [Bromberg-
Martin et al.,  2010].  The elevated dopamine binds  to

5.1  Experimental Setup
3
We employ the GPT-4o model  as the MLLM agent. In fact,
we have tested DeepSeek [Liu et al., 2024a], which currently
cannot receive audio as input and thus fails to support our
task. Based on EMOSpeech [Xu et al., 2024] and ESD [Zhou
et al., 2021] emotional speech datasets, we conduct a rigorous
selection process in terms of speech quality, emotional ex-
pression, and alignment between speech emotions and text.
This results in the creation of an emotion-rich dataset with
300 text-speech pairs in both Chinese and English, which
serves as our retrieval database for prosody retrieval.
To evaluate the performance of audiobook generation, we
conduct comprehensive tests on the Dopamine Audiobook
across various input combinations, scenarios, and user needs.
The evaluation is based on three perspectives:  (1) existing
speech generation metrics,  including DNSMOS [Reddy et
al., 2022], PLCMOS [Diener et al., 2023], SHEET [Huang
et al., 2024b], UTMOS [Saeki et al., 2022], ScoreQ [Ragano
et al., 2024], and NISQA [Mittag et al., 2021], which assess
speech quality, clarity, naturalness, and intelligibility through
predicted scores; (2) human-annotated Mean Opinion Score
(MOS), using the consistent scoring scheme as GPT; and (3)
our proposed evaluation framework.
To assess  the effectiveness of  the proposed evaluation
framework, we recruited five human annotators who were ad-
equately trained to ensure unbiased and discriminative rat-
ings.  Each annotator assessed over 200 generated speech
from various models across 30 scenarios, yielding authentic

5.2  Result and Analysis for Audiobook Generation
Comparison with SOTA Methods
We compare the performance of Dopamine Audiobook with
five leading models in controllable TTS: E2-TTS [Eskimez et
al., 2024], F5-TTS [Chen et al., 2024b], CosyVoice 2 [Du
et al., 2024b], VoxInstruct  [Zhou et al., 2024],  and Fir-
eRedTTS [Guo et al., 2024], as shown in Table 1.
Our method achieves the best results on the SHEET and
NISQA metrics, which measure speech quality, clarity, and
naturalness.  On the PLCMOS metric, Dopamine Audio-
book slightly lags behind F5-TTS. However, in human sub-
jective preference scores, Dopamine Audiobook outperforms
all other TTS models, with a final score of 3.685, while
others score around 3.1 or lower.  Furthermore, in terms of
speech expressiveness, Dopamine Audiobook significantly
surpasses existing methods,  thanks to our proposed flow-
based framework and two emotional enhancement strategies
that adaptively enhance emotional and paralinguistic infor-
mation based on user needs and scenarios. We also evalu-
ate the performance of different models within our proposed
evaluation framework, as shown in Figure 3. Dopamine Au-
diobook achieves the best results across various metrics, with
smaller confidence intervals, demonstrating its ability to reli-
ably produce high-quality outputs with enhanced stability.

Ablation Study: Audiobook Generation
We further explore the impact of different components on
Dopamine Audiobook’s performance, with experimental re-
sults shown in Table 1.  The proposed emotional enhance-
ment strategies, including word-level paralinguistic augmen-
tation and utterance-level prosody enhancement, effectively
improve the human-likeness and emotional expression of the
generated speech, leading to significant improvements in both
speech MOS metrics and human preference scores.  These
strategies, combined with our proposed flow-based frame-
work, enable finer-grained expression of speech with com-
plex emotional shifts and more natural speech with diverse
emotional dimensions by decomposing complex speech into
simpler emotion-level sub-sentence generation. Additionally,
the Adaptive Capability-based Model Selection module al-
lows the model to leverage the unique strengths of different
models through collaboration.
5.3  Result and Analysis for Evaluation Method
Comparison with Existing Metrics
We compare the evaluation performance of Dopamine Audio-
book with existing speech MOS metrics, as shown in Table 2.
Our framework outperforms current speech evaluation met-
rics in timbre alignment, expressiveness, and final score. No-
tably, Dopamine Audiobook demonstrates human-consistent
preferences for fine-grained emotional expression, while ex-
isting metrics fail to account for expressiveness.  In terms
of quality and naturalness, our method achieves comparable
performance to existing evaluation metrics.  It is important
to note that, since the multimodal GPT we use is scalable,
there is potential for our proposed evaluation framework to
improve further with advancements in GPT capabilities and
the development of other MLLMs..

In this work, we proposes Dopamine Audiobook, a training-
free MLLM agent for synthesizing and evaluating emotion-
ally expressive, human-like audiobooks. By utilizing a flow-
